attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of facial pustules. The lesions are painful and primarily affect her chin and jawline. She reports that the lesions worsen during her menstrual cycle. She has no significant medical history and takes the combined oral contraceptive pill. She works as an accountant and reports increased work stress over the past three months, leading to increased smoking (40 cigarettes per day) and poor sleep.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple inflammatory papules and pustules on the lower face and jawline. Several open and closed comedones are visible. There is no background facial erythema or flushing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of adult acne, including inflammatory papules and pustules primarily affecting the lower face and jawline. The presence of comedones (blackheads and whiteheads) is pathognomonic for acne vulgaris. The premenstrual flare and age of onset are typical for adult female acne, which often persists into or develops during adulthood. Contributing factors include smoking, stress, genetic predisposition, increased sebaceous secretion, and inflammation.

Rosacea typically presents with central facial erythema, flushing, and telangiectasia, often affecting the cheeks, nose, and central face. While inflammatory papules and pustules can occur in rosacea, comedones are notably absent, and the distribution pattern would be different.

Perioral dermatitis presents as small erythematous papules and pustules around the mouth, sparing the vermillion border, often with scaling and mild irritation. There are no comedones, and it is frequently associated with topical steroid use.

Folliculitis presents as inflammatory pustules centered on hair follicles and can occur anywhere on the body. While it can affect the face, it typically has a more random distribution and lacks comedones. The lesions are usually more superficial than those seen in acne.

Seborrhoeic dermatitis presents with erythematous patches with greasy scaling, typically affecting the nasolabial folds, eyebrows, and scalp. It does not present with pustules or comedones as the primary feature.

The management of adult acne often requires a combination approach, including topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, and/or topical antibiotics as with acne vulgaris presenting during puberty. Hormonal therapy (such as spironolactone or combined oral contraceptive pills) or oral antibiotics are second line treatment after failure of topical treatment or if the severity is moderate to severe.
learningPoints: Adult acne commonly presents with inflammatory papules and pustules in the lower face and jaw area.
The presence of comedones is a distinguishing feature of acne that is not seen in rosacea.
Premenstrual flares and smoking are common triggers for adult female acne.
references: Bagatin E, Freitas THP, Rivitti-Machado MC, Machado MCR, Ribeiro BM, Nunes S, Rocha MADD. Adult female acne: a guide to clinical practice. An Bras Dermatol. 2019 Jan-Feb;94(1):62-75. doi: 10.1590/abd1806-4841.20198203. Erratum in: An Bras Dermatol. 2019 Mar-Apr;94(2):255.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360964/

Cook D, Krassas G, Huang T. Acne: Best Practice Management. Australian Family Physician. 2010: 39 (9). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/41d41870-eba3-46c6-a7bf-26f2a3e2905f/Acne-best-practice-ma...
